mission.title: Install and Configure Ingress NGINX Controller
mission.description: The Ingress NGINX Controller is a Kubernetes Ingress controller that uses NGINX as a reverse proxy and load balancer. It is suitable for managing external access to services in a Kubernetes cluster. However, it is important to note that this project is retiring, and users are encouraged to migrate to a Gateway API implementation.

steps.title: Add the Ingress NGINX Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Ingress NGINX Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add ingress-nginx https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/main/deploy/helm-chart/ingress-nginx

```

> This URL points to the official Helm chart repository.

steps.title: Install the Ingress NGINX Controller
steps.description:
This command installs the Ingress NGINX Controller in the 'ingress-nginx' namespace using the specified version.

```bash

helm install ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx --namespace ingress-nginx --create-namespace --version 4.10.1

```

> Ensure the namespace is created if it doesn't exist.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Ingress NGINX Controller pods are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx

```

> You should see the Ingress NGINX Controller pods in a Running state.

steps.title: Create an example Ingress resource
steps.description:
This command creates a sample Ingress resource to test the Ingress NGINX Controller.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
  namespace: default
spec:
  rules:
  - host: example.local
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: example-service
            port:
              number: 80
EOF

```

> Replace 'example.local' and 'example-service' with your actual service details.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
  namespace: default
spec:

EOF

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Ingress NGINX Controller
uninstall.description:
This command removes the Ingress NGINX Controller and all associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if there are any Ingress resources that are not backed up.

```bash

helm uninstall ingress-nginx --namespace ingress-nginx

```

> This will remove the Ingress NGINX Controller from the cluster.

uninstall.title: Delete the Ingress NGINX namespace
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the namespace used for the Ingress NGINX Controller, which may leave orphaned resources.

```bash

kubectl delete namespace ingress-nginx

```

> Ensure that you have removed all resources before deleting the namespace.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Ingress NGINX Controller
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your Ingress resources. This step will upgrade the Ingress NGINX Controller to the specified version.

```bash

kubectl get ingress --all-namespaces -o yaml > ingress-backup.yaml

helm upgrade ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx --namespace ingress-nginx --version 4.10.1

kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx

```

> Backup existing Ingress resources.
> Upgrade to the specified version.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: '502 Bad Gateway'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates that the backend service is not reachable.

**Fix:**
Check the service and pod status with 'kubectl get svc' and 'kubectl get pods'.

troubleshooting.title: Ingress resource not working as expected
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Ingress resource may not be correctly configured.

**Fix:**
Review the Ingress resource configuration with 'kubectl describe ingress <ingress-name>'.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may be due to misconfiguration or resource limits being exceeded.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n ingress-nginx' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Ingress Controller not responding
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Ingress NGINX Controller may not be running.

**Fix:**
Ensure the Ingress NGINX Controller pods are running with 'kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx'.

versionNotes.changes: Updated to support Kubernetes 1.26, added new annotations for custom NGINX configurations.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain legacy annotations for better clarity.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Review the updated annotations and adjust your Ingress resources accordingly.

resolution.summary: You should see the Ingress NGINX Controller pods running with a status of 'Running' when you execute 'kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx'.

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.25 or higher and have kubectl and Helm installed.
